title: Lesson 4: Overview of the Bible
description: Welcome to a guided tour of the most important book you will ever read. The Bible isn't a single book. It is a library of 66 unique volumes written by 40 authors across 1,500 years. Learning how this life-changing book is organized will help you understand it and remember how the story of God fits together.

blocks.text: In the last lesson, you explored what it means to truly belong to a church family, discovering that the Christian life is not meant to be lived alone. But what holds that family together? It is the Word of God. In the next three lessons you are going to get to know that Word in a way you never have before. Think of this series as the essential gear for your journey: this lesson gives you the map, the next lesson takes you on the road trip, and the final lesson hands you the tools to dig into any passage on your own.
A Library, Not a Single Book

blocks.alt: Open Bible on a wooden table

blocks.text: Because the Bible is a library and not a single book, its contents are incredibly diverse. There are kings, shepherds, fishermen, doctors, tax collectors, and prophets among the writers. They wrote in different countries, in different centuries, in different circumstances. And yet the whole library tells one unified story from beginning to end, as we will learn in the next lesson.
That is remarkable. No human editor could have planned this across fifteen centuries. This unity is one of the strongest evidences that God Himself was behind every page. As Paul wrote to Timothy:
All Scripture is breathed out by God and profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness, that the man of God may be complete, equipped for every good work.

"Breathed out by God." That means when you open the Bible, you are not reading someone's personal journal or philosophical opinion. You are reading words that the living God intended for you to have. And because He organized this library with purpose, understanding how it is structured will help you read it with much greater confidence.

Some people wonder whether the Bible can really be trusted after being passed down for so long. Did it get distorted like a game of telephone over thousands of years? The answer is no, and here is why. In the ancient world, Scripture was read aloud to entire communities, not whispered from one person to the next. There were many witnesses in the room, not one. Scribes copied manuscripts with extraordinary care.

Here is the proof: the Dead Sea Scrolls were written over 200 years before Jesus was born and hidden in caves near the Dead Sea. When a shepherd boy discovered them in 1947, scholars compared them to the oldest copies we had been using, which dated to around 1000 AD. That is a gap of over a thousand years, and the texts matched almost word for word. The Bible stands alone as one of the most historically reliable documents of the ancient world. You can trust it with confidence.
4.1 - On a scale of 1 to 10, how well would you say you know the Bible right now? What is one thing you are hoping these next three lessons help you understand better?
Old Testament & New Testament

The Bible is divided into two major parts: the Old Testament and the New Testament. The word "testament" means covenant, a binding promise between God and His people. The Old Testament tells the story of God creating the world, choosing a people (Israel), giving them His law, and promising that a Savior would come. The New Testament tells the story of that Savior arriving in Jesus, the birth of the church, and the future hope of all things being made new.

The Old Testament has 39 books. The New Testament has 27. Together they form the complete Word of God. Some people struggle with the Old Testament because it feels distant or confusing. But here is the key: the Old Testament is not a different story with a different God. It is the first part of the same story, and every page of it points forward to Jesus. You cannot fully understand the New Testament without the Old, and you cannot fully understand the Old without the New.

Here is one more reason to trust the Old Testament: Jesus did. He quoted it constantly, argued from it, and treated every word of it as completely authoritative. If the eternal Son of God read those pages and accepted them without reservation, that is all the endorsement they need.
Below is every book of the Bible arranged on a shelf by section. Tap any book to learn what its section is about.
4.2 - After exploring the Bible bookshelf above, which section stands out to you most and why? Is there a shelf that most interests you?
More Than a Map

You have the map now. But the Bible was never meant to stay on a shelf. One of the most famous passages in all of Scripture comes from the very first section you explored, the Law. Moses spoke these words to Israel just before they crossed into the Promised Land:

Hear, O Israel: The LORD our God, the LORD is one. You shall love the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your might. And these words that I command you today shall be on your heart. You shall teach them diligently to your children, and shall talk of them when you sit in your house, and when you walk by the way, and when you lie down, and when you rise.

This is the Shema, and Jesus called it the greatest commandment. If you walked through the Growing course, you already know these words from Lesson 1, where you learned that loving God is not a burden but a response to a Father who loved you first. Now you know exactly where that passage lives - Deuteronomy, the Law section, the first shelf on your bookshelf. That is what knowing the map does. It connects what you have already learned to where it lives in Scripture.

Notice what Moses says to do with God's Word: put it on your heart, teach it to your children, talk about it when you sit down and when you get up, when you are home and when you are on the road. This is not a book to study once and set aside. It is meant to become part of how you think, how you talk, and how you live - and when you do, God promises that it will go well with you.
It is no empty word for you, but your very life, and by this word you shall live long in the land that you are going over the Jordan to possess.

No empty word. Your very life. Jesus echoed this same truth when Satan tempted Him in the wilderness. You can find it in Matthew 4, right on the Gospels shelf: man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word that comes from the mouth of God. The life that food gives you keeps you going for a few more hours. The life that God's Word gives you is firm, abundant, and never runs out. That Word is open in front of you right now.

blocks.alt: Open Bible, notebook, and coffee on a table

blocks.text: 4.3 - What would it look like for God's Word to become part of your everyday life - not just something you read, but something written on your heart?
A Word on Translations

The Bible was originally written in Hebrew (most of the Old Testament), Aramaic (small portions), and Greek (the New Testament). Because most of us do not read those languages, we rely on English translations. There are many good ones, and choosing a translation can feel confusing. Here is a simple way to think about it.

Some translations prioritize word-for-word accuracy, staying as close to the original language as possible. The ESV (English Standard Version) and NASB are examples of this. Others prioritize thought-for-thought clarity, translating the meaning of each sentence in a way that feels more natural to a modern reader. The NIV and NLT are examples of this. Both approaches are faithful to Scripture. A word-for-word translation is excellent for study. A thought-for-thought translation is excellent for reading and understanding quickly.

This course uses the ESV, which gives you a reliable, readable translation that stays very close to the original words. Whatever translation you use, the most important thing is that you are reading the Bible consistently. The best translation is the one you will actually open.
Lesson 4 Review

You now have the map. The Bible is a library of 66 books arranged in clear sections: Law, History, Poetry, Prophets, Gospels, Acts, Letters, and Revelation. The Old Testament tells the story of God creating, choosing, and promising. The New Testament tells the story of God fulfilling every promise in Jesus. In the next lesson you will take the road trip through the one big story that ties all of these books together.
Closing Reflections
